section: ABSTRACT
text: In the MSRE reactor vessel, fluid fuel is circulated at 1200 gpm down through an annular region and up through 1140 passages in the graphite core.

section: ABSTRACT
text: It also describes how the measurements were extrapolated to molten salt at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the actual reactor.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: The MSRE (Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment) is a 7.3 MW fluid-fueled, graphite-moderated, single region nuclear reactor.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: The nominal operating temperature is $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: INTRODUCTION
text: A detailed description of the reactor concept and its components is available in many sources, for instance References 1, 2 and 3.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: Most of the experimental results presented here were obtained in the early 1960's.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: This report was not written, however, until after the MSRE nuclear operations were terminated in December of 1969.

section: DESCRIPTION OF MSRE CORE AND TEST PROGRAM
text: Figure 1 shows an isometric view of the MSRE core.

section: DESCRIPTION OF MSRE CORE AND TEST PROGRAM
text: The fuel enters the reactor vessel at 1200 gpm through a constant flow area volute near the top of the cylindrical section.

section: DESCRIPTION OF MSRE CORE AND TEST PROGRAM
text: The moderator region is composed of long graphite core blocks, square in cross section, and with grooves cut longitudinally in the 4 faces.

section: DESCRIPTION OF MSRE CORE AND TEST PROGRAM
text: Figure 2 shows an isometric and a plan view of a small cluster or core blocks.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: The first phase consisted of building a 1/5 linearly scaled plastic model and testing with water.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: The only data presented in this report will be from the full scale model, however, a brief description of the 1/5 scale model and the way it was used is given in the next section.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: It was planned to operate the reactor at flow rates below the design flow of 1200 gpm.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: The lowest flow rate was undefined but could have been as low as $25\%$ of design flow.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: Later in the design stage, this reduced flow specification was dropped and design flow rate was fixed at 1200 gpm.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: As a result, data were taken at flow rates ranging from 1200 gpm to 300 gpm, but the emphasis in this report is on the 1200 gpm data.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: The purpose of these special runs was to study Xe-135 behavior.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: The "worst case" as far as lateral temperature gradients in the core is concerned, would be when it was operated at 5.5 MW (thermal) at half the design flow rate for a period of about 3 l/2 days.

section: Figure 2. Typical Graphite Core Block Arrangement
text: Figure 3 is a list of reactor parameters and physical properties of interest in this study.

section: One-Fifth Scale Model
text: A small transparent plastic model of the MSRE core, linearly scaled down by a factor of 1/5, was built and tested.

section: One-Fifth Scale Model
text: A photograph of the model is shown in Figure 4.

section: One-Fifth Scale Model
text: The particular scale factor of $1/5$ was chosen because a geometrically similar model of that size tested with water at about $95^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and at a flow rate such that its fluid velocities are equal to those of the reactor, will have the same Reynolds Number as that in the actual reactor core when

section: Full Scale Model
text: Figure 5 is a photograph of this model.

section: Full Scale Model
text: The core blocks were made by extruding aluminum approximately to shape including the longitudinal grooves and then taking only a finish cut on the $4$ side surfaces.

section: Figure 5. Full Scale Core Model
text: All the initial data from the full scale model was taken with the loop filled with water and operated between $75^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $80^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Figure 5. Full Scale Core Model
text: Later in the program a thickening agent (Jaguar-508 by Stein, Hall & Co.) was added to the system in order to simulate Reynolds Numbers, and the measurements which were strong functions of the Reynolds Number, were repeated.

section: Figure 5. Full Scale Core Model
text: Although Jaguar-508 imparts non-Newtonian characteristic to the water, in the low concentrations that were used in these tests, this was a negligible consideration.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: The main fuel loop piping in the MSRE is 5 in.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: Just prior to entering the core vessel volute the pipe size is increased to 6 in.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: The cross sectional flow area of the 6 in. pipe is approximately equal to that of the volute, 28.8 in.² and 26.0 in.², respectively.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: The orifices are 3/4 in. and occur in stacks of 3.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: At the head end of the volute the orifice stacks are 5 deg apart.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: At the tail end of the volute, because of the lower fuel velocity and resulting higher static pressure, the orifice stack spacing is increased to 22 1/2 deg.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: This orifice distribution was determined from the 1/5 scale

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: The orifice holes are drilled at an angle of 30 deg with the tangent in order to maintain a tangential velocity component in the annulus.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: Figure 6 is a plot of the experimentally measured centerline velocities in the volute as a function of angular position and at various flow rates.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: At 1200 gpm water approaches the core through the 5 in. pipe at a mean velocity of 19.2 ft/sec.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: Immediately inside the volute the velocity jumps to about 23 ft/sec because of recirculation around the volute.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: At the tail end of the volute the velocity is about 10 ft/sec.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: At the head end of the volute the mean velocity through the orifices is about 4 ft/sec and at the tail end of the volute the mean velocity through the orifices is about 18 ft/sec.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: Figure 7 is a plot of the centerline velocity in the core wall cooling annulus as a function of elevation.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: Figure 8 is a plot of the centerline velocity at the bottom of the core wall cooling annulus as a function of angular position around the core.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: Data for Figures 6, 7 and 8 was taken with water in the loop.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: The Reynolds Numbers involved in the volute and core wall cooling annulus are so high (over $10^{4}$ in all cases at 1200 gpm) that exact Reynolds similarity is not important, and the reactor vessel containing fuel salt will have the same velocity profiles.

section: Volute and Core Wall Cooling Annulus
text: At the midplane of the core (about 30 in. up in the annulus) and at 1200 gpm, the fluid velocity in the annulus is 7.2 ft/sec (Figure 7).

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: The lower plenum of the core vessel is formed by a standard 60 in.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: The anti-swirl vanes consist of 48 plates starting about 2 in. up in the core wall cooling annulus and extending along radial lines into the lower head for about $38\%$ of the radial distance to the core centerline.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: The vessel drain consists of a short section of 1 l/2 in. pipe extending slightly up into the vessel head at the centerline, and having a conical umbrella over it.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: In addition it incorporates a secondary drain which is a 1/2 in. concentric tube coming up the middle of the primary drain, penetrating through

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: the drain pipe wall just inside the vessel and wrapping around the drain pipe horizontally about $90^{\circ}$ .

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: The 1/2 in. line serves as a safety drain since it is designed to prevent slow migration of fuel in and out.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: Figure 9 is a plot of the experimentally observed radial pressure gradient as measured by wall pressure taps.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: Since the flow in the lower head is 3-dimensional, the static pressure at the wall is not an absolute measure of the pressure influencing flow through the moderator region, nevertheless it is a good indication.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: Figure 10 is a profile of this jet measured at a radius of 17 inches and at $\frac{1}{4}$ positions $90^{\circ}$ apart.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: The flow rate was 1200 gpm.

text: The equivalent diameter would be $\frac{1}{4}$ times the distance from the wall to the peak velocity.

section: Reactor Vessel Lower Head
text: The calculation with the Dittus-Boelter equation yields a heat transfer coefficient for fuel salt of 540 Btu/hr-ft $^{2}$ -F.

section: Figure 9. Radial Pressure Gradient At Wall Of Lower Vessel Head
text: Heat transfer coefficients were measured at a radius of 17 in. and 4 in. with water in the loop and were checked with water thickened with Jaguar for Reynolds Number similarity and found equal.

section: Figure 9. Radial Pressure Gradient At Wall Of Lower Vessel Head
text: More confidence is placed in the data at 17 in. than at 4 in. because the flow is well defined at the larger radius and less defined and gusty at the shorter radius.

text: It is expected that the predicted values of these heat transfer coefficients are conservative because they do not include the effect of thermal convection which is expected to contribute roughly 100 Btu/hr-ft² -°F to the overall coefficient.

text: These measurements indicate that the power oscillations originating from this mechanism should be well below $0.01\%$ of the mean power.

section: Graphite Moderator Assembly
text: The support structure consists of two assemblies, the lower of which is a Hastelloy-N cross structure (Figure 1) which is the main support structure for the graphite.

section: Graphite Moderator Assembly
text: The resulting square passages in the graphite grid are small and the fuel velocity is high (approx. $4 \frac{1}{2} \text{ft/sec}$ ), and therefore its pressure drop is high.

section: Graphite Moderator Assembly
text: Comparatively, the salt velocity through the bulk moderator fuel channels is low (approx. $0.7 \text{ft/sec}$ ) and its pressure drop is low.

section: Graphite Moderator Assembly
text: Figure 12 shows the experimentally determined head loss across the moderator assembly as measured in the model.

section: Graphite Moderator Assembly
text: Note that the slope of the curves in Figure 12 is 2.0 as would be expected since the Reynolds Number with water is over 4000 in the fuel channels and the bulk of the head loss is due to form losses in the graphite grid.

section: Graphite Moderator Assembly
text: The Reynolds Number in the fuel channels would be approximately 1000 so the flow character would theoretically be laminar, however, much of the turbulence generated by fuel passing through the tortuous inlet configuration would persist through the fuel channels.

section: Graphite Moderator Assembly
text: As a result one might expect a very slightly lower slope to the curves in Figure 12 with fuel salt in the system.

section: Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: The fuel then moves radially to the center and leaves the core through a 10 in. outlet pipe.

section: Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: After the model was built, a strainer structure (Figure 1) was added to the reactor outlet design which penetrated down into the upper plenum.

section: Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: Velocity profiles estimated by this technique appear in Figure 14.

section: Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: At the rated flow rate (1200 gpm), the Reynolds Number in the upper head is high for both water and fuel salt and the flow is turbulent.

section: Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: Assuming parallel plate geometry where the equivalent diameter is twice the channel width, the estimated heat transfer coefficient is a little over 100 Btu/hr-ft²°F neglecting thermal convection.

section: Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: The flow rate in this circuit has been estimated to be between 25 and 50 gpm depending on certain manufacturing and assembly tolerances.

text: Estimated temperatures in this region are presented in Reference 9.

section: Miscellaneous Measurements
text: The overall head loss through the core vessel from the 5 in. inlet line to the 5 in. outlet line was measured and is shown in Figure 15.

section: Miscellaneous Measurements
text: The curve has a slope of 2.0 indicating that the head loss is primarily due to form drag as expected, and very little due to skin friction.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: The experiments consisted of adding 2 or 3 pounds of sized iron filings to the water at the core inlet while the pump was running.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: They were added slowly over a specified period of time, generally about 20 minutes.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: The pump was then turned off either immediately after the addition or left running for an additional period up to 8 hours.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: The solids passed through the core only once because downstream of the core model was the 5400 gallon surge tank in which the particles would most certainly settle out.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: All data were taken at 1200 gpm, mostly with water but some runs with thickening agent added for Reynolds Number simulation.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: A summary of the data is shown in Figure 16.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: A particle diameter of 200-300 microns seems to be critical, that is, particles larger than this settled out in significant quantities, and particles smaller than this passed through the system.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: The gusty nature of the flow around the drain line assembly also seems to have some ability to re-suspend particles less than 300 microns in size if the pump is kept running after the addition phase.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: In no case was significant quantities of solids found in the 1 l/2 in. drain pipe.

section: Figure 14. Fluid Velocity Profiles In Reactor Vessel Upper Head
text: Because of the way the model was built, it was not possible to check the 1/2 in. emergency drain tube after each run.

section: EXPERIENCE WITH THE MSRE
text: Time reactor was critical - 17,655 hours

section: EXPERIENCE WITH THE MSRE
text: Nuclear heat production - 13,172 equivalent full power hours

section: EXPERIENCE WITH THE MSRE
text: Salt circulating in fuel loop - 21,788 hours.

section: EXPERIENCE WITH THE MSRE
text: The only instrumentation that could yield fluid dynamic performance information in the core was 64 thermocouples spotted at various locations on the outside of the core vessel (not in wells).
